data.source: Online participant pool (Amazon Mechanical Turk) across four laboratory-style survey experiments with real and hypothetical pay-offs.
data.access_details: Participants recruited and compensated through MTurk; no external public dataset specified.
data.notes: Comprehension checks and instructional manipulation checks used; some participants excluded for failing checks.

method.description: Across four between-subjects experiments, participants stated either their maximum willingness to pay (WTP) or minimum willingness to accept (WTA) for gift-certificate prospects whose label (e.g., lottery ticket, gift certificate, raffle, voucher, coin flip, gamble) and payoff structure were experimentally manipulated. Responses were elicited with incentive-compatible procedures (random selection of winners who could transact at a hidden reservation price).
steps: Recruit U.S. adults on Amazon MTurk and obtain consent.
Randomly assign participants to framing conditions and, where applicable, endowment status (buyer vs seller) and payoff structures.
Present instructions describing the prospect (e.g., ‘lottery ticket will for sure give either a $10 or $20 gift card’).
Administer an instructional manipulation/comprehension check; allow only correct respondents to continue.
Elicit monetary valuation (WTP or WTA) via open numeric entry.
Randomly select a subset of participants for real payoff implementation according to Becker-DeGroot-Marschak style mechanism.
Analyze data using t-tests, ANOVA and non-parametric (Mann–Whitney, Kruskal–Wallis) tests comparing means across frames, outcome levels and status.
method.models: Independent-samples t-tests; factorial ANOVA (e.g., 2×3×2 design); non-parametric Mann–Whitney U and Kruskal–Wallis tests.
method.outcome_variable: Monetary valuation: Willingness to Pay (WTP) or Willingness to Accept (WTA) for the described prospect.
method.independent_variables: Frame label (gift certificate, voucher, lottery ticket, raffle, coin flip, gamble); Endowment status (buyer vs seller); Payoff structure (low and high outcome amounts); Objective uncertainty (certain vs risky).
method.control_variables: None explicitly indicated beyond random assignment; demographic measures collected post-task.

results.summary: Across experiments, framing prospects with risk-associated labels (lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble) significantly lowered WTP compared with non-risk labels (gift certificate, voucher). WTA was unaffected by framing but increased with both low and high outcome magnitudes, whereas WTP depended mainly on the low outcome. The differential framing effect produced instances of the ‘uncertainty effect’ where a risky prospect was valued below its worst outcome when frames mismatched.

metadata.original_paper_title: Framing Influences Willingness to Pay but Not Willingness to Accept
